perturbation.original_text: (b) During the Restricted Period, Kennedy shall not permit any of its Affiliates to, directly or indirectly, hire or solicit any employee of the Company or encourage any such employee to leave such employment or hire any such employee who has left such employment, except pursuant to a general solicitation which is not directed specifically to any such employees; provided, that nothing in this Section 1 shall prevent Kennedy or any of his Affiliates from hiring (i) any employee whose employment has been terminated by the Company before the Merger, (ii) any employee whose employment has been terminated by the Company after the Merger one-hundred eighty (180) days from the date of termination of employment; or (iii) Jesse Miramontes (a current employee of the Company) and any Kennedy family member (including, without limitation, Nancy Kennedy (wife) or John C. Kennedy, IV (son) who are currently employed by the Company).
perturbation.changed_text: (b) During the Restricted Period, Kennedy shall not permit any of its Affiliates to, directly or indirectly, hire or solicit any employee of the Company or encourage any such employee to leave such employment or hire any such employee who has left such employment, except pursuant to a general solicitation which is not directed specifically to any such employees; provided, that nothing in this Section 1 shall prevent Kennedy or any of his Affiliates from hiring (i) any employee whose employment has been terminated by the Company before the Merger, (ii) any employee whose employment has been terminated by the Company after the Merger thirty (30) days from the date of termination of employment; or (iii) Jesse Miramontes (a current employee of the Company) and any Kennedy family member (including, without limitation, Nancy Kennedy (wife) or John C. Kennedy, IV (son) who are currently employed by the Company).
perturbation.explanation: Changing the waiting period before Kennedy can hire a former employee of the company from 180 days to 30 days could violate non-solicitation agreement enforceability standards. Courts often find long restrictions more reasonable.
perturbation.law_explanation: Michigan law requires that non-compete agreements be reasonable in duration and geographic scope. Shortening the waiting period to 30 days might be deemed unreasonable depending on the employee's position and the specific facts of the case, making the non-solicitation clause unenforceable. The one-hundred eighty (180) days may be more reasonable than thirty (30) days.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: MCL - Section 445.774a - Michigan Legislature MCL - Section 445.774a Download Section Chapter 445 Act 274 of 1984 Previous Section Next Section MICHIGAN ANTITRUST REFORM ACT (EXCERPT) Act 274 of 1984 445.774a Agreement or covenant protecting business interests of employer; applicability of section. Sec. 4a. (1) An employer may obtain from an employee an agreement or covenant which protects an employer's reasonable competitive business interests and expressly prohibits an employee from engaging in employment or a line of business after termination of employment if the agreement or covenant is reasonable as to its duration, geographical area, and the type of employment or line of business. To the extent any such agreement or covenant is found to be unreasonable in any respect, a court may limit the agreement to render it reasonable in light of the circumstances in which it was made and specifically enforce the agreement as limited. (2) This section shall apply to covenants and agreements which are entered into after March 29, 1985. History: Add. 1987, Act 243, Imd. Eff. Dec. 28, 1987
perturbation.justification: The change from a 180-day to a 30-day waiting period before Kennedy can hire former employees introduces a legal contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation suggests this change might violate enforceability standards for non-solicitation agreements. The Law Explanation states that Michigan law requires non-compete agreements to be reasonable in duration. Snippet 1, MCL 445.774a, reinforces this by stating that such agreements must be 'reasonable as to its duration'. Reducing the waiting period to 30 days could be deemed unreasonable, potentially rendering the non-solicitation clause unenforceable under Michigan law. The original 180-day period is more likely to be deemed reasonable than the reduced 30-day period. This qualifies as an 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' because the time-sensitive obligation was changed, making the contractual terms potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: (a) For a period of three (3) years commencing on the Closing Date (the “Restricted Period”), Kennedy shall not, nor shall Kennedy permit any of his Affiliates, directly or indirectly, (i) engage in or assist others in engaging in the Autocam Business; (ii) have an interest in any Person that engages directly or indirectly in the Autocam Business in any capacity, including as a partner, shareholder, member, employee, principal, agent, trustee, or consultant; or (iii) intentionally interfere in any material respect with the business relationships (whether formed before or after the date of this Agreement) between the Company and customers or suppliers of the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: (a) For a period of ten (10) years commencing on the Closing Date (the “Restricted Period”), Kennedy shall not, nor shall Kennedy permit any of his Affiliates, directly or indirectly, (i) engage in or assist others in engaging in the Autocam Business; (ii) have an interest in any Person that engages directly or indirectly in the Autocam Business in any capacity, including as a partner, shareholder, member, employee, principal, agent, trustee, or consultant; or (iii) intentionally interfere in any material respect with the business relationships (whether formed before or after the date of this Agreement) between the Company and customers or suppliers of the Company.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-compete period from three years to ten years makes it more likely to be considered unreasonable and unenforceable under state law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Michigan law requires that non-compete agreements be reasonable in duration. A ten-year non-compete period is highly likely to be deemed unreasonable, especially considering the context of the agreement and the nature of Kennedy's involvement after the merger. A court will likely not enforce this for the full 10 years.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: MCL - Section 445.774a - Michigan Legislature MCL - Section 445.774a Download Section Chapter 445 Act 274 of 1984 Previous Section Next Section MICHIGAN ANTITRUST REFORM ACT (EXCERPT) Act 274 of 1984 445.774a Agreement or covenant protecting business interests of employer; applicability of section. Sec. 4a. (1) An employer may obtain from an employee an agreement or covenant which protects an employer's reasonable competitive business interests and expressly prohibits an employee from engaging in employment or a line of business after termination of employment if the agreement or covenant is reasonable as to its duration, geographical area, and the type of employment or line of business. To the extent any such agreement or covenant is found to be unreasonable in any respect, a court may limit the agreement to render it reasonable in light of the circumstances in which it was made and specifically enforce the agreement as limited. (2) This section shall apply to covenants and agreements which are entered into after March 29, 1985. History: Add. 1987, Act 243, Imd. Eff. Dec. 28, 1987
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the non-compete period from 3 years to 10 years. The Perturbation Explanation states that this change makes the agreement more likely to be considered unreasonable and unenforceable under state law. The Law Explanation supports this, stating that a ten-year non-compete is likely unreasonable. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically MCL - Section 445.774a, states that a non-compete agreement must be 'reasonable as to its duration'. A ten-year restriction is likely to be viewed as unreasonable, thus creating a contradiction of law.

perturbation.original_text: (d) During the Restricted Period, Kennedy shall provide written notice as soon as reasonably possible (it being agreed that ninety (90) days prior written notice is acceptable) before (i) Kennedy or any of his Affiliates begin to engage in the Company Business excluding the medical device business or (ii) Kennedy or any of his Affiliates enter into a transaction to acquire the stock or substantially all of the assets of any Person that engages in the Company Business excluding the medical device business.
perturbation.changed_text: (d) During the Restricted Period, Kennedy shall provide written notice thirty (30) days before (i) Kennedy or any of his Affiliates begin to engage in the Company Business excluding the medical device business or (ii) Kennedy or any of his Affiliates enter into a transaction to acquire the stock or substantially all of the assets of any Person that engages in the Company Business excluding the medical device business.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the written notice period from a 'reasonable time' (defined as 90 days) to 30 days before engaging in a competing business, may be too short for the company to adequately protect its interests. Making it potentially challengeable in court.
perturbation.law_explanation: Michigan law states non-competes should be reasonable. A 30 day notice is not reasonable if it is insufficient to allow the company to properly respond to Kennedy's new business venture. A court might find the 90 day term more reasonable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: MCL - Section 445.774a - Michigan Legislature MCL - Section 445.774a Download Section Chapter 445 Act 274 of 1984 Previous Section Next Section MICHIGAN ANTITRUST REFORM ACT (EXCERPT) Act 274 of 1984 445.774a Agreement or covenant protecting business interests of employer; applicability of section. Sec. 4a. (1) An employer may obtain from an employee an agreement or covenant which protects an employer's reasonable competitive business interests and expressly prohibits an employee from engaging in employment or a line of business after termination of employment if the agreement or covenant is reasonable as to its duration, geographical area, and the type of employment or line of business. To the extent any such agreement or covenant is found to be unreasonable in any respect, a court may limit the agreement to render it reasonable in light of the circumstances in which it was made and specifically enforce the agreement as limited. (2) This section shall apply to covenants and agreements which are entered into after March 29, 1985. History: Add. 1987, Act 243, Imd. Eff. Dec. 28, 1987
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the notice period from 90 days to 30 days, which could be deemed unreasonable under Michigan law concerning non-compete agreements. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that non-compete agreements must be 'reasonable as to its duration, geographical area, and the type of employment or line of business'. The Law Explanation supports the argument that 30 days might not be sufficient for the company to respond to Kennedy's new venture, implying unreasonableness.

perturbation.original_text: (f) All notices, requests, consents, claims, demands, waivers, and other communications hereunder shall be in writing and shall be deemed to have been given (i) when delivered by hand (with written confirmation of receipt); (ii) when received by the addressee if sent by a nationally recognized overnight courier (receipt requested); (iii) on the date sent by facsimile or e-mail of a PDF document (with confirmation of transmission) if sent during normal business hours of the recipient, and on the next Business Day if sent after normal business hours of the recipient or (iv) on the third (3rd) day after the date mailed, by certified or registered mail, return receipt requested, postage prepaid.
perturbation.changed_text: (f) All notices, requests, consents, claims, demands, waivers, and other communications hereunder shall be in writing and shall be deemed to have been given (i) when delivered by hand (with written confirmation of receipt); (ii) when received by the addressee if sent by regular mail (receipt not required); (iii) immediately on the date sent by facsimile or e-mail of a PDF document (with confirmation of transmission) regardless of the time sent; or (iv) on the fifth (5th) day after the date mailed, by regular mail, return receipt not requested, postage prepaid.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the notice provisions to allow for regular mail without requiring receipt and making email notices effective immediately may be deemed inadequate and unfair, especially for critical legal notices. This could violate due process requirements related to notice.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware contract law implies a duty of good faith and fair dealing. Using regular mail without receipt confirmation and immediate effectiveness of email, especially outside of business hours, may be seen as bad faith. The original terms allowed for a higher probability that the recipient would receive it during working hours and provides proof of that receipt.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 6 > Article 1 Authenticated PDF General Provisions Part 1. General Provisions Part 2. General Definitions and Principles of Interpretation Part 3. Territorial Applicability and General Rules
perturbation.justification: The change from requiring receipt confirmation for mail to allowing regular mail without receipt introduces a significant weakening of proof of notice, which can contradict the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. The original text ensures a higher probability of receipt and provides proof, while the changed text does not. While the snippet only provides the title of a section of Delaware law, the Law Explanation points out that Delaware contract law implies a duty of good faith and fair dealing. Using regular mail without receipt confirmation and immediate effectiveness of email, especially outside of business hours, may be seen as bad faith because it undermines the probability that the recipient would receive it.

perturbation.original_text: In the event any suit or proceeding against Kennedy to enforce any of the provisions of this Agreement or on account of any damages sustained (or alleged to have been sustained) is brought by the Company by reason of Kennedy’s violation or alleged violation of any of the provisions of this Agreement, the parties agree that, in addition to other costs and damages, all reasonable costs and attorneys’ fees incurred by the party prevailing in such action shall be paid by the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event any suit or proceeding against Kennedy to enforce any of the provisions of this Agreement or on account of any damages sustained (or alleged to have been sustained) is brought by the Company by reason of Kennedy’s violation or alleged violation of any of the provisions of this Agreement, the parties agree that, regardless of the outcome, Kennedy shall pay all of the Company’s costs and attorneys’ fees.
perturbation.explanation: Requiring Kennedy to pay the Company's legal fees regardless of the outcome of the suit is against public policy. This is because it discourages a person from defending themselves in court. Kennedy may have a legitimate claim, and will now be required to pay the other's legal bills anyway.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware contract law does not encourage provisions that deter individuals from defending themselves in court. While fee-shifting provisions are permissible, one that requires the defendant to pay regardless of the outcome is an unreasonable constraint on access to justice and runs contrary to public policy. The party prevailing should have its legal fees paid, as this encourages proper behavior.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 6 > Article 1 Authenticated PDF General Provisions Part 1. General Provisions Part 2. General Definitions and Principles of Interpretation Part 3. Territorial Applicability and General Rules
perturbation.justification: The modification in the contract text creates a legal contradiction because it requires Kennedy to pay the Company's legal fees regardless of the outcome of the suit. This contradicts the principle that parties should not be deterred from defending themselves in court. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that such a provision is an unreasonable constraint on access to justice and runs contrary to public policy, even though fee-shifting provisions are permissible. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 generally references Delaware Code, the Law Explanation elaborates that deterring individuals from defending themselves is against public policy.
